title: The Beam Width Tradeoff
concept: Bigger beam width B finds better sentences but requires more memory/compute; B=3 to 5 is a typical choice in production.

exercises.prompt: How does beam search help an AI find better translations?

options.text: It only looks at one word at a time.
It explores multiple translation paths at once.

exercises.explain: Beam search tracks multiple options at the same time to find the best overall translation, unlike greedy search which only looks at one.

cards.title: Too Many Choices?
cards.body: When AI translates sentences, it has to choose how many options to keep in mind at once. Keep too few, and the translation becomes messy. Keep too many, and your app slows to a crawl! How do AI engineers find the perfect balance?

cards.title: What is Beam Width?
cards.body: Beam Width (represented as B) is the number of translation paths the AI tracks at the same time. While a larger width finds higher-quality sentences, it demands much more memory and processing power. In the real world, a beam width of 3 to 5 is the industry standard balance.

cards.title: The Travel Agent's Desk
cards.body: Imagine shopping for flight tickets. If you only look at the absolute cheapest flight at each step (Beam Width = 1), you might miss a cheaper overall round-trip deal. If you search through every flight combination ever (huge Beam Width), you'll crash your browser. Searching 3 to 5 options at a time is the sweet spot to find the best deal without freezing your computer.

cards.title: Width in Action
cards.body:
An AI translation system translates 'Bonjour' with different beam widths:
• B=1 (Greedy): Fast, but might pick 'Good day' even if 'Hello' sounds more natural later.
• B=4 (Standard): Keeps 4 paths open. It takes slightly longer but translates it beautifully.
• B=100 (Overkill): Keeps 100 paths open. The translation is identical to B=4, but it takes 20 times longer to load.

cards.title: The 'Infinite Width' Myth
cards.body: Many people assume that setting the beam width as high as possible (like 100 or 1000) will always give much better translations. In reality, beyond a width of 5, the quality rarely improves, but the computer memory usage skyrockets. Bigger isn't always better!

cards.title: The Beam Width Tradeoff
cards.body: Here is how different beam widths affect performance and resource usage.

left.text: Super fast, uses very little memory, but often misses the best translation.

right.text: Very slow, uses massive memory, but finds high-quality sentences.

exercises.prompt: What is the typical range for beam width (B) used in real-world production translation systems?
exercises.explain: A beam width of 3 to 5 is the standard sweet spot used in production systems to balance quality and speed.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Using a larger beam width increases the computer memory needed to translate a sentence.
exercises.explain: A larger beam width allows the AI to evaluate more options simultaneously, which increases memory and compute usage.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe the tradeoff of a larger beam width.
exercises.sentence: A larger beam width finds better sentences but requires more memory and compute.
exercises.explain: Increasing the beam width allows the AI to consider more options, but it requires much more memory and compute power.

exercises.prompt: Three AI assistants are designing a chat translator. Choose the assistant that recommends the best balance.

options.text: AI Assistant 1: 'Let's use a beam width of 100 to make sure we don't miss a single word combination.'
AI Assistant 2: 'Let's use a beam width of 4. It will give us great translations and keep our app running fast.'
AI Assistant 3: 'Let's set the beam width to 1 so the model only looks at one word and runs as fast as possible.'

exercises.explain: AI Option 2 is correct because it uses a balanced beam width of 4. Option 1 uses an excessively high beam width (100) which wastes resources, and Option 3 uses a beam width of 1 which is just greedy search.

exercises.prompt: You are launching a real-time translation app. Users want translation results in milliseconds. Which beam width (B) should you choose to balance speed and accuracy?

options.text: B = 1 (greedy search) to save all CPU power
B = 100 to ensure the absolute perfect translation every time
B = 3 to 5 to get high quality without lagging the app
B = 0 to disable search and let the model guess randomly

exercises.explain: A beam width of 3 to 5 balances quality and speed. B=1 is too sloppy (greedy search), while B=100 is too slow and uses too much memory for real-time translation systems.
